lessons.title: What is compound interest?
lessons.content:
Compound interest is the interest on a loan or deposit calculated based on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods. 

Think of it as 'interest on interest'. It will make a sum grow at a faster rate than simple interest, which is calculated only on the principal amount.

The rate at which compound interest accrues depends on the frequency of compounding, such that the higher the number of compounding periods, the greater the compound interest.

Over long periods, compound interest can significantly multiply your wealth, which is why starting to save early is highly recommended.

quiz.question: What is compound interest?
Which grows faster?
options: They grow at the same rate

quiz.question: How does compounding frequency affect the total?

lessons.title: How EMI works
lessons.content:
Equated Monthly Installment (EMI) is a fixed payment amount made by a borrower to a lender at a specified date each calendar month. 

Equated monthly installments are applied to both interest and principal each month so that over a specified number of years, the loan is paid off in full.

In the initial years of a loan, a larger portion of the EMI goes towards paying the interest. As time passes and the principal reduces, the interest component decreases and the principal repayment component increases.

Understanding EMI helps you plan your monthly cash flows better and avoid taking loans you cannot afford.

quiz.question: What does EMI stand for?
In early years, what makes up most of the EMI?
Does EMI amount change every month for a fixed rate loan?

lessons.title: 50-30-20 Budgeting Rule
lessons.content:
The 50/30/20 rule is a simple and practical budgeting framework. It suggests dividing your after-tax income into three categories of spending: 50% for needs, 30% for wants, and 20% for savings or paying off debt.

Needs are your absolute essentials like rent, groceries, basic utilities, and minimum debt payments.

Wants include dining out, entertainment, subscriptions, and travel.

Savings include adding to your emergency fund, investing for retirement, and paying extra towards debt.

This rule provides a clear guideline, but you can adjust the percentages based on your specific financial situation.

quiz.question: What does the 50 represent in the 50/30/20 rule?
What category does dining out fall under?
Can the 50/30/20 rule be adjusted?
options: Only if you earn over 1 Lakh

lessons.title: Emergency Fund Basics
lessons.content:
An emergency fund is a bank account with money set aside for large, unexpected expenses. Examples include medical bills, major car repairs, or sudden job loss.

A good rule of thumb is to save 3 to 6 months' worth of essential living expenses. If you spend ₹10,000 a month on needs, aim for an emergency fund of ₹30,000 to ₹60,000.

Keep this money in a highly liquid account, like a high-yield savings account or fixed deposit that can be broken easily, so you can access it quickly when needed.

Having an emergency fund prevents you from going into high-interest debt when bad things happen.

quiz.question: What is an emergency fund for?
How much should ideally be in an emergency fund?
Where should you keep an emergency fund?

lessons.title: Good Debt vs Bad Debt
lessons.content:
Not all debt is bad. 'Good debt' is money owed for things that can help build wealth or increase income over time, such as student loans (investing in your earning potential) or a mortgage (buying an appreciating asset).

'Bad debt' involves borrowing money to purchase rapidly depreciating assets or for consumption. Examples include high-interest credit card debt used for vacations or expensive clothes.

While good debt has lower interest rates and tax benefits, bad debt has very high rates that can trap you in a cycle of repayment.

Always evaluate the return on investment before taking on any debt.

quiz.question: Which of these is typically considered 'Good Debt'?
options: Credit card debt for a vacation
Car loan for a luxury vehicle

quiz.question: What characterizes 'Bad Debt'?
options: High interest rates for depreciating assets

quiz.question: Should you avoid all debt completely?
options: No, some debt can help build wealth
Only if you are a student

lessons.title: How to read a loan statement
lessons.content:
A loan statement provides a snapshot of your loan account. Key details to check include the Principal Outstanding (how much you still owe), Interest Rate, and the EMI amount.

Look for the breakdown of your last payment: how much went to principal and how much to interest. Over time, the principal portion should increase.

Also, check for any penalties, late fees, or bounce charges. If you see unrecognized charges, contact your bank immediately.

Reviewing your statement helps you ensure payments are correctly applied and tracks your progress toward becoming debt-free.

quiz.question: What does Principal Outstanding mean?
Why check the breakdown of your payment?
options: To see if interest rate changed
To verify how much principal was reduced

quiz.question: What should you do if you see a late fee but paid on time?

lessons.title: Savings rate and why it matters
lessons.content:
Your savings rate is the percentage of your gross income that you save each month. If you earn ₹20,000 and save ₹4,000, your savings rate is 20%.

Your savings rate is the most important factor in determining how quickly you can build wealth and achieve financial independence. A high income with a 0% savings rate still leaves you broke.

Increasing your savings rate involves either decreasing your expenses or increasing your income (and not inflating your lifestyle).

Aim for at least a 20% savings rate, but pushing it higher will accelerate your financial goals dramatically.

quiz.question: How is savings rate calculated?
What happens if you have a high income but 0% savings rate?
How can you increase your savings rate?

lessons.title: Student loan repayment strategies
lessons.content:
Repaying student loans effectively requires strategy. Firstly, always make at least the minimum EMI payment on time to protect your credit score.

Consider paying more than the EMI. Any extra payment goes directly to reducing the principal, which significantly lowers the total interest you will pay over the loan term.

If you have multiple loans, use the 'Avalanche Method' (pay extra on the highest interest rate loan first) or the 'Snowball Method' (pay off the smallest balance first for psychological wins).

Also, check if you can claim tax deductions on the interest paid under Section 80E (in India), which effectively reduces the cost of the loan.

quiz.question: What happens when you pay extra on top of your EMI?
What is the Avalanche Method?
options: Paying off highest interest loans first

quiz.question: Under which section can you claim tax deductions on education loan interest in India?
